text: The surgeon uses intraoperative colonoscopy to confirm the location of the submucosal lesion, placing the endoscope directly at the level of the mass situated at the anti-mesenteric border of the colon.

The surgeon uses a laparoscopic clipapplier to mark the region of interest. Robotic ports are placed along the patient's right side, and the small bowel is swept away from the colon. The robotic system is docked at the patient's left hip. The marked area of the lesion is visible, and a longitudinal incision is made in the colon using a monopolar hook.

The surgeon continues dissection until the lumen of the colon is visualized, noting thermal spread from the instrument. A suction evacuation system maintains clear visualization. Dissection proceeds along the medial border of the submucosal mass, with the assistant using a grasper and robotic instrument to provide traction for cautery. The mucosa overlying the lesion appears severely inflamed due to the lipoma, prompting a full thickness dissection that includes the inflamed mucosa.

The surgeon continues dissection close to the submucosal lesion using a monopolar hook, noting the instrument's performance and thermal spread. The hook lacks precision compared to an ultrasonic dissector, and a scissor with cautery might offer better precision.

The surgeon dissects the mass away from the subserosal plane, positioning the lipoma adjacent to the colon. The colotomy is held open, and full thickness stay sutures are placed in the lateral portion of the colon to prepare for a tension-free transverse closure. The descending colon is gently dissected away from the lateral sidewall.

The surgeon uses the third robotic arm to hold a stay suture up toward the abdominal wall. Another suture is placed in the mid portion of the colotomy and tied down in preparation for a stapled closure. After securing the suture with several knots, the needle is cut and removed from the abdomen. A third full thickness stitch is performed at the medial portion of the colotomy, with the suture grasped and held up toward the abdominal wall. The robotic stapler is introduced into the abdomen.

The surgeon uses the medial stay suture to achieve the proper angle for closure. The medial portion of the colotomy is visibly closed as the stapler is fired. Another stay suture is placed to approximate the two sides of the colotomy, aiming to minimize colon removal during the stapled closure. The suture is secured with several knots, and the needle is removed from the abdomen. The stapler is reintroduced, and with the help of the assistant grasper and stay sutures, the correct angle is obtained for stapling.
The surgeon closes the stapler, which visibly cracks through the inflamed colonic tissue at the site affected by the lipoma. A stay suture is placed to approximate the colotomy edges.

The surgeon uses the stapler to perform an excellent closure of the colotomy, now positioned away from the inflamed colonic tissue. The assistant grasper aids in dynamically adjusting the stay suture and anastomotic edge, ensuring proper angulation for the stapler to safely continue the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses stay sutures and the anastomotic edge to reposition the colon, minimizing excision and preventing stenosis during colotomy closure. The stapler is employed to complete the closure, highlighting the technical difficulty of this operation.

The surgeon places the lipoma and anastomotic edge into an endocatch bag, closes the bag, and removes the specimen from the abdomen. The operative field is inspected, showing a closed staple line without bleeding or stenosis.
